administration: Indication: cardiac arrest Use concentration 0.1mg/ mL
concentration_note: Caution: IV use in cardiac arrest
particularity: In cardiac arrest secondary to VF/VT: • Prioritize defibrillation over epinephrine. • Consider waiting after the second defibrillation before giving the first dose of epinephrine. • Consider a maximum dose of 3mg total in cases of refractory VF/VT.

administration: Indication: IM anaphylaxis Use concentration 1mg / mL  IM in the thigh < 1 min
concentration_note: Caution: IM use in anaphylaxis
particularity: After three IM doses, evaluate the need for an epinephrine infusion.

administration: Presentation: ampoules of 1ml (1mg/mL) 1) Add 4mg (4mL) or 8mg (8mL) to 250mL of NS 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Final concentration : 15.7mcg/mL or 31mcg/mL 4) Start infusion at the minimal dose of 0.01mcg/kg/min 5) Titrate by increments of 0.01-0.03mcg/kg/min q3-5min as needed (t1/2 <5min) (expert opinion)  N.B. Increase starting dose and titrate faster depending on degree of instability.
particularity: The minimal dose of 0.01mcg/kg/min was chosen to limit the chance of overdosing this medication in the obese population. Adjust according to hemodynamic response. If no response at doses greater than 0.1mcg/kg/min, evaluate the need for an alternative therapy.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Presentation: ampoules of 1mL (1mg/mL) 1) Add 4mg (4mL) or 8mg (8mL) or 16mg (16mL) or 32mg (32mL) to 250mL of NS 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Final concentration: 15.7mcg/mL or 31mcg/mL or 60mcg/mL or 113mcg/mL 4) Start infusion at the minimal dose of 0.01mcg/kg/min 5) Titrate by increments of 0.01-0.03mcg/kg/min q3-5min as needed (t1/2 <5min) (expert Opinion)  N.B. Increase starting dose and titrate faster depending on degree of instability.
particularity: The minimal dose of 0.01mcg/kg/min was chosen to limit the chance of overdosing this medication in the obese population. Adjust according to hemodynamic response.  There is no consensus in the literature on the maximum dose of epinephrine. The maximum dose is an editorial choice.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site. For higher concentrations (16mg and 32mg in 250mL), use central venous access.

administration: Nebulization of the required dose without dilution
particularity: Pediatric evidence supports its use in laryngitis. Some sources suggest its use for edema of the ENT sphere or acute severe asthma (Off-label). There is no literature regarding a maximal dose in terms of safety.

administration: Presentation: cardiac epinephrine 0.1mg/mL (1mg in 10mL) 1) Withdraw 0.1mg (1mL) of epinerphrine 2) Add to 9ml of NaCl 0.9% 3) Final concentration: 0.01mg/mL = 10mcg/mL 4) Clearly identify syringes with the correct concentration 5) Administer 0.005-0.02mg = 5-20mcg (0.5-2mL) IV q1-5min PRN
particularity: Little evidence supports the use of push-dose pressors.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Indication: IM refractory acute severe asthma Use concentration 1mg / mL  IM in the thigh < 1 min
particularity: GINA guidelines recommend the use of IM epinephrine in asthma when associated with anaphylaxis or angioedema.  The literature tends to show that systemic epinephrine as a bronchodilator is not more effective than inhaled or nebulized bronchodilators. Use only in refractory cases.  After three IM doses, evaluate the need for an epinephrine infusion.

administration: Presentation: - MAD-300 device - Luer-Lock syringe 1 or 3mL - Fentanyl 50mcg /mL  1) Nasal toilet before administration as needed. 2) Draw the desired dose and add 5mcg (0.1mL) to fill the dead space. 3) Connect the MAD-300 device to the syringe. 4) Divide the dose evenly between the two nostrils (possible volume of 0.2-1mL per nostril). 5) Administer over 1 to 2 seconds.
particularity: Consider reducing the dose by 50% if: - Patient > 70 years old. - Pulmonary, cardiovascular or neurological comorbidities. - Intoxication. - Regular use of central nervous system depressants.  Protect ampoules from light.
reference_snippet: - Belkouch A et al. Does intranasal fentanyl provide efficient analgesia for renal colic in adults? Pan Afr Med J. 2015 Apr 24;20:407  - Judith Finn et al. A randomised crossover trial of patient controlled intranasal fentanyl and oral morphine for procedural wound care in adult patients with burns.

administration: Administer 3 to 5minutes before induction. Administer IV undiluted over 1 to 2 minutes.
particularity: No definitive evidence supports the use of fentanyl in rapid sequence intubation. May be used to reduce the sympathetic response to intubation in patients with increased intracranial pressure or in whom it is desired to avoid an increase in blood pressure.  May increase the risk of peri-intubation hypotension. Adjust the dose according to the clinical situation. Doses of 0.5 to 4 mcg/kg are described in the literature.  Protect ampoules from light.
reference_snippet: - Ferguson I, Buttfield A, Burns B, Reid C, Shepherd S, Milligan J, Harris IA, Aneman A; Australasian College for Emergency Medicine Clinical Trials Network. Fentanyl versus placebo with ketamine and rocuronium for patients undergoing rapid sequence intubation in the emergency department: The FAKT s

administration: Administer the non-diluted form (50mcg/mL) or the infusion solution with pump (10mcg/mL) over 1 to 2 minutes.
particularity: Use minimal required dose for adequate analgesia. The use of a standardized tool for pain evaluation in intubated patients is strongly recommended (ex: CPOT). Fentanyl is usually dosed based on ideal body weight. Enter patient height for more precise and adequate dosing.  Protect ampoules from light.

administration: Presentation: 50mcg/mL 1) Remove 50mL from a 250mL bag of NaCl 0.9%. 2) Add 2500mcg (50mL) of fentanyl to the remaining 200mL of NaCl 0.9%. 3) Final concentration: 10mcg/mL. 4) Start at desired rate (usually 0.35-1.5mcg/kg/h). 5) Titrate by increments of 0.25-0.5mcg/kg/h q30-60min as needed (t1/2 > 2 hours) (expert opinion). 6) IV boluses can be administered during the infusion in case of acute pain. 7) Although the maximum quoted dose is 3mcg/kg/h, doses up to 10mcg/kg/h are described in the literature.

preparation: Every 3 to 5 minutes PRN, maximum 3 mg total

administration: Caution: multiple concentrations available.  Double the dose every 5 minutes until muscarinic signs resolve (bronchorrhea, bradycardia, hypotension).   Consider starting an atropine infusion.

administration: Caution: multiple concentrations available. Dilute dose in a compatible solution to achieve a final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, administer using a volumetric pump.  Examples using syringes with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL: • Transfer contents into an empty bag or syringe that can adapt to a syringe pump or volumetric pump. •  Final concentration: 0.1 mg/mL.  Examples using an ampoule with a concentration of 0.4 mg/mL: • 5 mg (12.5 mL) in 37.5 mL of NaCl 0.9%. Final volume: 50 mL. Final concentration: 0.1 mg/mL. • 10 mg (25 mL) in 75 mL of NaCl 0.9%. Final volume: 100 mL. Final concentration: 0.1 mg/mL.  Examples using an ampoule with a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL: • 5 mg (8.3 mL) in 41.7 mL of NaCl 0.9%. Final volume: 50 mL. Final concentration: 0.1 mg/mL. • 10 mg (16.7 mL) in 83.3 mL of NaCl 0.9%. Final volume: 100 mL. Final concentration: 0.1 mg/mL.
particularity: To maintain atropinization, a continuous IV infusion may be started at 10% to 20% of the total loading dose every hour (maximum initial rate of 3 mg/h). • Infusion rate should be titrated up and down depending on signs of under or over atropinization. • There is no maximum cumulative dose of atropine in treatment of organophosphorus or carbamate insecticide toxicity.

administration: Administration may be irritating. Administer in a proximal vein when possible.  If using the lipid formulation, shake the ampoule before withdrawing the dose.
particularity: Dose reduction is recommended for elderly patients or patients with renal or hepatic impairment. Maximum doses are an editorial choice based on the fact that etomidate should be dosed based on ideal body weight.  Can cause myoclonus.

administration: Use a proximal peripheral venous access.  Two administration options: 1) Administer the dose of adenosine IV push and flush immediately with 10 to 20mL of NaCl 0.9%. OR 2) Dilute the dose of adenosine with NaCl 0.9% for a total volume of 20mL and administer IV push with a single syringe.
particularity: In a stable patient, consider vagal maneuvers first.  Reduce the dose by 50% if:  • Administered via central venous access • Transplanted heart • Concomitant carbamazepine use

administration: Presentation: 50mg/mL 1) Add the desired dose to 100mL of D5% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Administer IV over 10 minutes Can be administered over 30 minutes for less urgent situations.  Must be administered with a 0.22 micron IV tubing filter. This can be omitted for the bolus in emergency situations.
particularity: May be administered more quickly depending on the clinical situation (maximum speed: 30mg/min).  Additional doses of 150mg can be administered every 10 minutes as needed for a maximum of 2200mg (including the infusion) for the first 24 hours.  The rate of cardioversion of AF/flutter with amiodarone is rather low and this agent can be used for rate control in hemodynamically unstable or precarious patients.

administration: 1) Administer 0.2 mg IV over 30 seconds. 2) If no clinical response after 60 seconds, administer 0.3 mg IV over 30 seconds. 3) If no clinical response after 60 seconds, administer 0.5 mg IV over 30 seconds. 4) Repeat the dose of 0.5 mg IV every 60 seconds as needed. 5) Maximum cumulative dose of 5 mg. Consider other etiologies for the clinical presentation if no clinical response after a cumulative dose of 3 mg. 6) Start the infusion if necessary.
particularity: Contraindicated: - Coma of unknown origin - Co-ingestion of another toxic substance - Epilepsy - Benzodiazepine dependence - Sodium channel blockade on EKG - Intubated patient

administration: IM in the thigh. Do not give more than 5mL per IM site. If the dose is greater than 250mg (5mL), use two injection sites.
particularity: Doses reported in the literature from 3 to 6mg/kg. Choose the dose according to the clinical situation, the co-administration of other molecules and the hemodynamic and respiratory status of the patient. A higher dose will result in faster and more effective sedation, but with the potential for more adverse effects.  Consider using IV or IM benzodiazepines as a first choice, depending on the clinical situation.  Protect non-reconstituted vials from light.

administration: Nil
particularity: Ensure adequate analgo-sedation is in place immediately after intubation to prevent a paralyzed patient not receiving analgesia and sedation.  There is no consensus in the literature on the maximum dose of rocuronium. The maximum dose chosen is an editorial choice: use of total body weight to a maximum of 100kg. Higher doses (>1.4mg/kg) possibly give better intubation conditions. However, the use of ideal versus total body weight remains controversial and higher doses lead to longer periods of paralysis.  Keep refrigerated  Reversal agent: sugammadex

indication: Acetaminophen intoxication, Quebec poison control protocol
administration: This is the protocol of the Quebec Poison Control Center  Weight less than or equal to 50 kg: 1) Use a 500 mL bag of D5%. 2) Do not remove solute. 3) Add 24,000 mg (120 mL) of NAC 200 mg/ml. 4) Administer 150 mg/kg over 1 hour (first hour) then 15 mg/kg/h for at least 20 hours or as recommended by the poison control center (see "infusion" step).  Weight greater than or equal to 51 kg (maximum weight 100 kg): 1) Use a 1000 mL bag of D5%. 2) Do not remove solute. 3) Add 48,000 mg (240 mL) of NAC 200 mg/ml. 4) Administer 150 mg kg over 1 hour (first hour) then 15 mg/kg/h for at least 20 hours or as recommended by the poison control center (see "infusion" step).
particularity: To be started and stopped according to the criteria of your poison control center.  Watch for the risk of an anaphylactoid reaction.  Calculate the dose based on actual weight up to a maximum of 100 kg.  Also compatible with NaCl 0.9% and 0.45%.

preparation: Administer without dilution with syringe driver
administration: Administer undiluted with syringe driver at a rate of 5 mg/min = 1 mL/min (50 mg dose over 10 minutes; 100 mg dose over 20 minutes). If a syringe driver is not available, the dose can be introduced into an empty bag and administered on pump at the same rate.  In case of elevated anion gap, but without metabolic acidosis: 1 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 6 hours until the intoxication resolves.  In case of elevated anion gap with metabolic acidosis: 2 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 4-6 hours until the intoxication resolves and the metabolic acidosis is corrected. Adjust the dose and the interval according to the severity of the acidosis and patient characteristics (denutrition, alcoholism, etc.).
particularity: Expert advice: Maximum dose for 1 mg/kg : 50 mg Maximum dose for 2 mg/kg : 100 mg

particularity: Consider in case of failure of medical treatment. The evidence does not demonstrate a reduction in global mortality, but rather a reduction in mortality secondary to bleeding. administration: Can be administered without dilution IV over 10 minutes with syringe driver.
particularity: The efficacy of tranexamic acid remains controversial in angioedema because of the lack of definitive studies. However, tranexamic acid is safe, widely available and easy to administer which makes it a therapy to consider for bradykinin-mediated angioedema.  For hereditary angioedema cases, administer 1st line agents when possible (e.g. Berinert, icatibant).

administration: Nil
particularity: Doses up to 15mg/kg have been reported in herpes zoster encephalitis.  Maintain adequate IV hydration during administration.  Fixed dose. Adjust the dosing interval according to renal fonction. Usual interval q8h.  Expert advice: Adjusted body weight is used for all weights (when inferior to total body weight) to ensure a logical progression in the doses with increasing patient weights. Some sources recommend using adjusted body weight only in patients with obesity (total body weight > 120% of ideal body weight or BMI > 30).

indication: Paracentesis; acording to quantity drained in Liters
administration: The dose is rounded to the nearest full vial to facilitate administration.  Administer with ventilated tubing. Administer according to local protocols. Usually, one vial of 25g (100mL) over 1 hour. The large amounts of volume can lead to circulatory overload.
concentration_note: 100 mL vials (25 g)
particularity: It is recommended to administer 8g of albumin for every liter of ascites drained, if drainage of more than 5L. In patients at high risk of hemodynamic or renal decompensation, a replacement can be administered for drainage of less than 5L with the same ratio of 8g for every liter drained.

administration: Reconstitution: 100mg vial -100 mg vial of alteplase -100 mL vial of Sterile Water for Injection (SWFI) - Transfer device  Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Mix the alteplase vial with the diluent with the help of the transfer device provided. DO NOT shake the bottle. 2) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 3) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 4) Final concentration: 1 mg/mL  Reconstitution: 50mg vial - 50 mg vial of alteplase - 50 mL vial of Sterile Water for Injection (SWFI) - 60 mL syringe - Large bore needle (18 gauge)   Refer to the monograph for detailed reconstitution 1) Using the syringe and the large needle, take 50 mL of the SWFI. Inject the SWFI into the alteplase vial. 2) Mix the solution with a gentle swirl to make sure the product is completely dissolved. 3) Visually inspect the solution for particulate matter and discoloration prior to administration. 4) Final concentration: 1mg/mL
concentration_note: Make sure there are no contraindications

particularity: Withhold heparin before beginning alteplase administration. Heparin can be restarted when the activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) value is less than 1.5-2 times normal.  Consider administering a bolus of 10-20mg IV push if the patient is very unstable. The rest of the dose can then be administered IV over 2 hours.  Some studies suggest that the 50mg dose is as effective as the 100mg dose. Consider reducing the dose in patients with lower weight (<60kg) or at higher risk of bleeding. A dose of 25mg over 6h can also be considered for patients at high risk of bleeding and/or with less instability as it might be as effective according to some studies.  *** This list is not exhaustive, see monograph ***  Absolute Contraindications: - Active internal bleeding - Within last 3 months: intracranial or intra-spinal surgery, severe head trauma, intracranial hemorrhage, prior ischemic stroke - Intracranial aneurysm, AVM, intracranial neoplasia - Known bleeding diathesis - Suspected aortic dissection  Relative Contraindications: - Age > 75 years - Current use of anticoagulants - Pregnancy - Traumatic CPR - Internal bleed within 4 weeks prior - Severe hypertension (> 180/110) - Dementia - Major surgery within 3 weeks prior - Ischemic stroke > 3 months old

dose_text: 1000 mg of amoxicillin / 200 mg of clavulanic acid
administration: *** Presentation: vial of amoxicillin 1000 mg / clavulanic acid 200mg or amoxicillin 500 mg / clavulanic acid 100mg = 5:1 ***  Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's information. The primary dilution (of the vial) is stable 15 minutes after dilution. The primary dilution may be slightly pink, this coloration should disappear after shaking.
concentration_note: Dose expressed in mg of amoxicillin
particularity: Unstable when diluted in dextrose solution. However, may be compatible with dextrose solution as secondary infusion.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual dose: 1000mg IV q8h   The 2g and 1g doses are not interchangeable since they contain different ratios of amoxicillin and clavulanic acid (10:1 vs 5:1)

administration: Presentation: 1 mmol/mL (50 mL syringe) 1) Add 150 mmol (150 mL) to 1 L of D5%. 2) Do NOT remove volume during preparation. 3) Final concentration: 130 mmol/L.  Start infusion at 2-3 mL/kg/h (initial maximum 200 mL/h) and titrate according to urinary pH.
particularity: Aim for urinary pH greater than 7.5. Aim for serum pH 7.5-7.55. Keep the serum potassium over 4 mmol/L.

route: Add 3 syringes of 50 mL of NaHCO3 to 1 L of D5%
administration: Presentation: 1 mmol/mL (syringes of 50 mmol in 50 mL) 1) Add 150 mmol (3 syringes) to 1 L of D5%. 2) Do NOT remove volume during preparation. 3) Final concentration: 130 mmol/L. 4) Titrate the infusion acording to potassium level and volume status.
particularity: This therapy may be effective only in the presence of metabolic acidosis and does not replace other therapies known to be effective in hyperkalemia (insulin, B2-agonists, calcium, etc.).  Some studies recommend the administration of 1 L of this solution over 4 hours (250 mL/h).  Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate therapy (non diluted ampules) does not seem to be effective in the treatment of hyperkalemia.

administration: *** Off-label ***  In the presence of a clinical response to the bolus dose, consider starting the infusion 1-2 hours after the bolus dose.  1) Add 250mg (25mL) to 100mL of D5%. 2) Final concentration = 2mg/mL. 3) Start the infusion at 0.5 mg/kg/h. 4) Increase by 0.25mg/kg/h q1h depending on the clinical response. 5) Maximum dose of 1mg/kg/h.
particularity: The infusion is optional for anaphylaxis and was only used in a minority of case reports. When used, the treatment time was usually 1-2 hours.  The usual maximum dose of methylene blue is 7 mg/kg of ideal weight in 24 hours.  Pay attention to interactions with serotonergic agents since severe toxicity can occur.  Pulse oximetry may no longer be reliable after administration of methylene blue.  If an infusion is started, administration by central route or intraosseous catheter is preferred (vesicant agent).

administration: Administer preferentially via central access only. Rinse tubing after each injection using 2.5 - 30mL of NaCl 0.9% to reduce the risk of thrombophlebitis.
particularity: The use of calcium gluconate is preferred in a stable patient with a peripheral access. Severe hypocalcemia: total serum calcium < 1.9mmol/L (7.5mg/dL) and/or symptomatic patient.  In case of concomitant hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia, giving calcium may cause precipitation of calcium phosphate (calciphylaxis). The ideal treatment in this situation is treatment of the hyperphosphatemia.

administration: Administer via central venous access.  Depending on the desired flow rate and the patient's weight, insert between 10g (100mL) and 20g (200mL) of calcium chloride 10% solution in an empty solute bag (e.g. Viaflex) and administer on a volumetric pump at the indicated flow rate. If not available, insert 5g (50mL) of calcium chloride 10% solution into an empty syringe and administer with a syringe pump at the indicated rate.  Discuss with your poison control center. Titrate the infusion rate according to the effectiveness of the treatment on blood pressure and the serum ionized calcium concentration (do not exceed an ionized calcium 1.5-2 times the upper limit of normal).
particularity: The use of calcium gluconate is preferred in stable patients with peripheral venous access only.  Calcium chloride 10% and calcium gluconate 10% solutions are physically stable at least 48 hours at room temperature (25 ºC, 60% relative humidity) in a polypropylene syringe and in an empty intravenous solute bag that contains PVC and DEHP according to the results of an investigators lead study with the financial support of the Quebec Poison Centre and the Pharmacy department of the CIUSSSCN.

administration: Administer preferentially via central venous access. If central access is not available, consider using calcium gluconate. Rinse tubing after each injection using 2.5 - 30mL of NaCl 0.9% to reduce the risk of thrombophlebitis. Do not administer in the same IV access as blood products.
particularity: Most patients with massive hemorrhage develop hypocalcemia. Blood product administration exacerbates this hypocalcemia by dilution and chelation because of citrate. administration: Administer undiluted IV over 2-5 minutes. Administer preferably through a proximal IV access (hyperosmolar solution). Rinse tubing after each injection using 2.5 - 30mL of NaCl 0.9% to reduce the risk of thrombophlebitis.
particularity: Administer only if suspicion of a reversible cause by the administration of calcium. Empirical administration of calcium for all cases of cardiac arrest is not supported by the literature and may be associated with reduced survival.  Several sources prefer the use of calcium chloride as first-line therapy in cardiac arrest as for the same amount of elemental calcium administered, less vials are needed with calcium chloride.

administration: 1 g/kg, maximum of 50 g per dose.  Shake vigorously before administration. Administer PO over 15 minutes or via NG tube.  For administration via NG tube, cut the tip of the bottle and insert the end of the probe inside. Gently squeeze the bottle to empty to content into the gastric tube.  After administration, rinse the content of the tube with 5 to 10 mL of sterile water for injection, then clamp the tube for a period of 1 to 2 hours.  Can be mixed with other liquids to improve the taste: Chocolate milk, volume ratio 1: 1. Coca-cola, volume ratio 1: 1. Cherry syrup, volume ratio 6: 1 (charcoal: syrup).
particularity: Discuss with your poison control center.  The use of activated charcoal in sorbitol suspension is no longer recommended.  Consider activated charcoal in a potentially toxic ingestion of less than an hour and if there are no contraindications (including airway protection). Discuss with your poison control center before administering via NG tube in an unintubated patient.

administration: Presentation: 0.5mg/mL (50 or 100mL vial) No preparation necessary.  Start the infusion at 1-2 mg/h. Double the infusion rate every 90 seconds initially. As BP approaches desired target, increase dose with smaller intervals (1-2mg/h) and every 5-10 minutes.  A majority of patients will require a dose of 16mg/h or less (4-6mg/h on average). Although some patients may require doses up to 32mg/h, doses of more than 21mg/h over 24 hours or more are not recommended due to the risks associated with the lipid load.

administration: 20 mg vials to be reconstituted with 60 mL sterile water. Concentration after reconstitution = 0.333 mg/mL. Administer IV push after reconstitution.  This concentration is the only one available on the Canadian market and involves a significant quantity of liquid. Administer IV push as rapidly as possible.
particularity: The loading dose may be repeated every 5 to 10 minutes until stiffness subsides or for a cumulative dose of up to 10 mg/kg.  Some patients may require a higher total dose. Discuss with you Poison Control Center.

administration: 1) Remove 20mL from a 100mL bag of 0.9% NaCl. 2) Add 2000mg of deferoxamine (4 vials of 500mg or 1 vial of 2000mg). 3) Start the infusion at 15mg/kg/h or a maximum of 1000mg/h as the initial dose (50mL/h). 4) Higher doses may be needed depending on the clinical situation and if tolerated by the patient. Discuss with your local toxicology center before increasing the infusion and to determine the total duration and discontinuation of treatment (administration of deferoxamin causes an underestimation of circulating iron levels; dose iron levels only after stopping the treatment). 5) Usual maximum dose of 6g per 24 hours.
particularity: Indications: - Ingestion of 40mg/kg or more of elemental iron with symptoms of toxicity. - Serum concentration greater than 90 mcmol/L (500mcg/dL) in acute intoxication. - Serum concentration greater than 63 mcmol/L (350mcg/dL) and cardiovascular symptoms or lactic acidosis.

indication: Bleeding associated with Hemophilia A or von Willebrand disease
administration: Dilute in 50mL of NaCl 0.9%. Administer IV over 20 minutes.
particularity: Keep refrigerated. May cause hyponatremia.  Indicated to treat minor bleeding only. Doses may be repeated thereafter (8 to 12h later, then daily), in which case free water intake should be limited and serum sodium monitored.  Some studies support the use of DDAVP in intracranial bleeding in patients on antiplatelet agents (0.4mcg/kg).

administration: Administer IV push. Doses of dexamethasone up to 20mg can be administered IV push without dilution.  Can also be administered PO at the same dose.
particularity: Recommended dose: 6 mg daily for 10 days (RECOVERY study). Reduced mortality in patients requiring oxygen therapy or invasive mechanical ventilation.  Use with Tocilizumab if possible. If Tocilizumab (or other similar agent) is not available, consider higher doses of dexamethasone (10 to 20mg daily). In patients with very severe hypoxemia, some experts recommend the use of higher doses of dexamethasone (10 to 20 mg die) regardless of other therapies.  In the event of a dexamethasone shortage, consider the use of other corticosteroids. Approximate equivalence: dexamethasone 0.75mg = methylprednisolone 4mg = prednisone 5mg.

administration: If 4 mcg/mL pre-prepared vials not available:  1) Use vials of 100 mcg/mL. 2) Remove 4 mL from a 100 mL bag of  0.9% NaCl. 3) Add 400 mcg (4 mL) of dexmedetomidine to 96 mL of 0.9% NaCl. 4) Final concentration = 4 mcg/mL.   Titration information:  1) Start the infusion at 0.4 mcg/kg/h for frail patients and at 0.7 mcg/kg/h for others. 2) Increase the infusion by 0.1 mcg/kg/h every 15-30 minutes until the target level of sedation is obtained (use a sedation scale, e.g. RASS). 3) Maximum recommended dose 1.4 mcg/kg/h.
particularity: Contraindicated in patients with severe heart failure, AV bloc or bradycardia. Monitor for the risk of hypotension and bradycardia.  Reduce the dose in patients with renal impairment, hepatic insufficiency.  In case of agitation, provide boluses of another agent (ex: propofol). Dexmedetomidine boluses are not recommended for this indication.  Off-label: can be used for other indications at the same doses (eg: sedation for non-invasive ventilation, adjunctive treatment in alcohol withdrawal, etc.).

indication: Adjuvant to high dose IV insulin in toxicology
administration: Administer a bolus of D10% of 250mL (or 0.5g/kg of D50%) before starting the infusion.  Always administer with volumetric pump.
concentration_note: D10% bag
particularity: Start only if blood sugar levels below 22mmol/L (400mg/dL) and aim for blood sugar levels above 5.5mmol/L (100mg/dL). Monitor blood glucose q30min up to a few hours after the end of the insulin infusion.  This concentration should be used via the peripheral route in the absence of central access. In the presence of central venous access, use an infusion of D50%. Considering the significant flow rate of the D10% solution, the transition to a more concentrated solution administered via central access should be done as quickly as possible.  In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose.

administration: Administer IV direct under pressure over 5 minutes or with a volumetric pump over 15 minutes depending on the clinical situation. Follow with an infusion if clinically indicated.  For less urgent situations, consider lowering the dose to 125mL to reduce the incidence of rebound hypoglycemia.
concentration_note: D10% bag
particularity: Equivalent dose of glucose (25g) to a 50mL ampoule of D50%.  Consider as an alternative to D50%: - Less rebound hypoglycemia - Less irritation at the site of injection - Less risk in case of extravasation  Consider a dextrose infusion and frequent glycemia measurements to prevent rebound hypoglycemia.

indication: Adjuvant to IV insulin in hyperkalemia
administration: Equivalent dose of glucose (25g) to a 50mL ampoule of D50%.
concentration_note: D10% bag
particularity: Administer dextrose concomitantly with IV insulin in hyperkalemia if blood glucose < 14 mmol/L (250mg/dL).  Consider a 2nd dose 30-60 minutes after insulin administration or an infusion of a dextrose-containing solution depending on blood sugar and risk of hypoglycemia (higher risk in CKD, smaller weight, older age, lower initial blood sugar).  Consider as an alternative to D50%: - Less irritation at the injection site - Less risk if extravasation

indication: Adjuvant to high dose IV insulin in toxicology
administration: Although a D50% ampoule can be delivered with a syringe driver, an average patient will need an ampule approximately every 45 minutes. It is therefore preferable to use commercially available D50% bags or to have a more concentrated solution prepared by pharmacy. If this is not possible, another option is to inject several ampules of D50% into an empty bag and infuse at the same rate.
particularity: Start only if blood sugar levels below 22mmol/L (400mg/dL) and aim for blood sugar levels above 5.5mmol/L (100mg/dL). Monitor blood glucose q30min up to a few hours after the end of the insulin infusion.  Administer a bolus of D50% of 0.5g/kg before starting the infusion.  This concentration should be used only via central access. In the absence of central venous access, use a D10% infusion. Consider obtaining central venous access as soon as possible since the administration of a D50% solution is preferable (smaller flow rate).  In the hemodynamically unstable patient, low blood sugar should be treated by increasing the dextrose intake and not by decreasing the insulin dose.

administration: Administer at 5mg/min IV bolus. Administer the 40mg dose by syringe-driver.
particularity: It is recommended to use a standardized assessment scale for alcohol withdrawal (CIWA or RASS). Use the smallest effective dose and increase as needed and based on clinical response. Very high doses can sometimes be necessary  Usual interval q20-60 min PRN. Depending on the clinical situation, the initial doses can be titrated rapidly upwards and given more closely (q5-10 min) under continuous monitoring.  Rapid sedation after the first doses of benzodiazepines should lead to questioning of the diagnosis of alcohol withdrawal.

preparation: Add to NaCl 0.9% for a total volume of 10 mL
administration: Choose an initial bolus of 0.25mg or 0.5mg. Dilute with NaCl 0.9% for a total volume of 10mL. Administer IV over 5 minutes.
particularity: After the initial bolus, repeat 0.25mg IV q6h for a maximum of 1.5mg total for the first 24h. Then, initiate the maintenance PO dose as clinically indicated.  In patients with older age, smaller weight and/or reduced CrCl, use smaller doses and consider a maximum dose of 0.75mg total for the first 24h.  Some sources recommend an IV loading dose of 8-12mcg/kg ideal body weight (6-10mcg/kg ideal body weight in renal insufficiency) given 50% as a loading dose, than 25% given twice every 6 hours.  Less effective in case of high sympathetic activity. Contraindicated in case of pre-excitation.

administration: 1) 1st dose 0.25mg/kg (max 20mg). 2) If ineffective after 15 minutes, administer a 2nd dose at 0.35mg/kg (max 25mg). 3) If ineffective after 15 minutes, a 3rd dose at 0.35mg/kg (max 25mg) may be considered, to be individualized according to the patient and the clinical presentation. 4) Consider a diltiazem infusion based on clinical response.
particularity: A strategy using 10mg boluses instead of higher doses based on weight might be appropriate for some patients (older patients and/or lower blood pressure).  Avoid in patients with hypotension and in case of acute heart failure or known LV dysfunction.  Use with caution in patients with sepsis or hemodynamic compromise.  Keep refrigerated.

administration: Administer undiluted or diluted up to 5 mL of D5%. Administer IV over 1 min.
particularity: The injectable formulation of haloperidol is not officially approved for IV administration, but is safe according to extensive clinical experience and large studies.  Dose can be repeated every 15 to 30 minutes as needed until the targeted level of sedation is achieved.  Watch for QTc prolongation, especially with doses of more than 20 mg over 24 hours.  For elderly patients in delirium without severe agitation, reduce the dose significantly (0.5-1 mg). Cumulative and higher doses of antipsychotics are associated with risk of falls and mortality.  Depending on the clinical situation, consider the concomitant use of a benzodiazepine. Decrease the dose of haloperidol accordingly because of increased risk of respiratory depression with this association.

particularity: The injectable formulation of haloperidol is not officially approved for IV administration, but is safe according to extensive clinical experience and large studies.  Dose can be repeated every 15 to 30 minutes as needed until the targeted level of sedation is achieved.  Watch for QTc prolongation, especially with doses of more than 20 mg over 24 hours.  For elderly patients in delirium without severe agitation, reduce the dose significantly (0.5-1 mg). Cumulative and higher doses of antipsychotics are associated with risk of falls and mortality.  Depending on the clinical situation, consider the concomitant use of a benzodiazepine. Decrease the dose of haloperidol accordingly because of increased risk of respiratory depression with this association.

indication: Diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperglycemic hyperosmolar syndrome
administration: Ensure that K+> 3.2mmol/L before initiating insulin.  Insulin binds to IV tubing: 1) Fill the tubing with the solution and clamp for 15 minutes. 2) Rinse the tubing with the insulin solution for 1 minute before connecting it to the patient. Step 1 can be omitted in emergency situations.
particularity: This represents an initial regimen of insulin use for DKA and HHS. IV fluids and insulin titration should be done according to hospital policy or protocol. Consider continuing basal insulin to facilitate transition to SC insulin upon resolution of the acute episode.  Keep refrigerated.

particularity: Prioritize the 5-unit dose in older patients or patients with advanced renal insufficiency: less hypoglycaemia and probably as effective as the 10-unit dose at lowering potassium.  Concomitantly administer dextrose if blood glucose lower than 14mmol/L (250mg/dL) and check blood glucose frequently after administration (at 15, 30, 60, 90, 120 minutes, than hourly). Post-insulin hypoglycaemia can occur up to 4-6 hours after administration (higher risk in CKD, smaller weight, older age, lower initial blood sugar). Consider an infusion of a dextrose-containing solution depending on the clinical situation.  Can be repeated 2-4h later if hyperkalemia persists despite other treatments.  Keep refrigerated.

preparation: Administer IV without dilution over 3 minutes
administration: Discuss with your local poison center.  Administer with NaCl 0.9% or D5% with "Y" derivation.  If anionic gap without metabolic acidosis: 1 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 6 hours until the intoxication resolves.  If anionic gap with metabolic acidosis: 2 mg/kg. Repeat the dose every 4-6 hours until the intoxication resolves and the metabolic acidosis is corrected. Adjust the dose and the interval according to the severity of the acidosis and history (denutrition, alcoholism, etc.).
particularity: Expert advice: Maximum dose for 1mg/kg: 50mg Maximum dose for 2mg/kg: 100mg

administration: More rapid administration can lead to serious side effects (visual changes, headache, seizures). It is therefore recommended to give the dose in 30 minutes.  The loading dose can be given diluted, but the concentration cannot exceed 8 mg/mL and the 6000 mg dose should therefore be diluted in 1 L of solution and administered over 30 minutes, which is not possible with most volumetric pumps. For this reason, the loading dose should be given via syringe driver without dilution.  If a syringe driver is not available, empty a 25 or 50 mL bag, introduce the dose into the empty bag and administer over 30 minutes.
particularity: To be considered as an adjuvant in acute valproic acid poisoning in a symptomatic patient (encephalopathy or presence of hepatoxicity), not undergoing hemodialysis, with hyperammonemia.  Maximum dose of 6 g.  Follow the loading dose with maintenance doses; start 4 hours after the loading dose.

administration: 1) Reconstitute each vial with 5 mL of 0.9% NaCl • vial of 200 mcg reconstituted: 40 mcg/mL • vial of 500 mcg reconstituted: 100 mcg/mL 2) Administer the desired dose IV at 100 mcg/min • 200 mcg over 2 minutes • 300 mcg over 3 minutes • 400 mcg over 4 minutes
particularity: Loading dose:  • Administer 200 mcg to patients of low body weight, older age or at greater risk of cardiac complications.  • Administer 400 mcg in younger patients, with higher body weight and without comorbidities.  If the IV form is not available, consider PO administration with a loading dose between 300 mcg and 500 mcg.  Consider the concomitant administration of T3 according to the clinical situation and with specialized advice. Administration of T3 may increase the risk of cardiac complications.  Administer with hydrocortisone in view of the high prevalence of concomitant adrenal insufficiency in these patients.

administration: Caution: multiple concentrations available.  Presentation 0.4 mg/mL: Administer 0.1 mg (0.25 mL) to 0.4 mg (1 mL) IV push every 2 to 3 minutes PRN. particularity: The primary survey (evaluation of the airway, the breathing and the circulation) should be prioritized over the administration of naloxone.  Naloxone is not recommended for in-hospital use when patients are intubated or during cardiac arrest due to an opioid.   After 10 mg of naloxone without clinical response, consider an alternate diagnosis and endotracheal intubation. However, a dose higher than the maximum recommended dose may be required to reverse the effects of synthetic opioids.

administration: Attention: multiple concentrations available.  Do NOT remove volume during preparation.   1) Calculate the dose corresponding to 2/3 of the cumulative effective bolus dose. 2) Start the infusion to administer this calculated dose in 1 hour. 3) Stop the infusion according to the clinical response and the half-life of the narcotic.  Example: The patient wakes up after 2 boluses of naloxone: 1st bolus 0.5 mg, 2nd bolus 1 mg. 1) Effective cumulative bolus dose = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5 mg. 2) 2/3 of this dose = 2/3 x 1.5 = 1 mg. 3) Start the infusion at 1 mg/h.
particularity: An infusion can also be started empirically at 0.2-0.4 mg/h. If respiratory depression reappears, a new loading dose of naloxone should be given and this dose repeated as needed, or continue the infusion.  The duration of the naloxone infusion should be dictated by the clinical picture, but also by the type of opioid involved, its dose and formulation (progressive release or not).

administration: If pre-prepared bag not available: Presentation: ampoules of 10 mg in 10 mL. 1) Remove 90 mL from a 250 mL bag of 0.9% NaCl = 160 mL. 2) Add 40 mg of nicardipine (4 vials = 40 mL) to the bag. 3) Final concentration = 40 mg in 200 mL = 0.2 mg/mL.   Titration information: 1) Start the infusion at 5 mg/h. 2) Titrate by 2.5 mg/h every 5-15 minutes until target BP is achieved (onset of action 5-10 min; rapid titration q5 min or gradual titration q15 min). 3) Maximum dose of 15mg/h. *** Caution: BP can drop precipitously after the initial titration *** 4) Once target BP is achieved, decrease the infusion to 3mg/h, then titrate again gradually as needed. 5) If BP falls below target, decrease the infusion to minimum rate or hold entirely, then titrate again gradually as needed.

particularity: The risk of cyanide toxicity increases with doses greater than 2mcg/kg/min if used over many hours. particularity: There is no consensus in the literature on the maximum dose of norepinephrine.  The maximum dose of 90mcg/min is an editorial choice.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site. For higher concentrations (16mg and 32mg in 250mL), use central venous access.

administration: 1) Take 5 mg of phentolamine. 2) Administer IV bolus over 1 minute. 3) Repeat the loading dose every 5 to 10 minutes PRN for a maximum of 4 doses (20 mg). 4) Maximum effect reached in 2 minutes, duration of action 15 to 30 minutes. 5) Always follow the loading dose with the infusion.

indication: Vitamin K antagonist (VKA) reversal in patients with acute major bleeding
preparation: Administration rate varies according to indication
administration: Use only if prothrombin complex concentrates (4-factor) are not available or contraindicated since fresh frozen plasma takes longer to administer, may cause transfusion reactions and has been shown to be inferior in the literature.  Administer according to local protocols and the urgency of the situation. Can be administered flush with pressure bag in case of major hemorrhage.
particularity: Administer 4 units empirically, or 2 units and repeat dose if INR remains above 1.5. Measure INR 15 minutes after the end of plasma administration.  Must be administered with Vitamin K (10mg IV) in case of major bleeding.  Consider furosemide prior to plasma administration in patients at risk for volume overload. Consider empirical calcium administration or close monitoring and repletion of calcium as need.

particularity: The dose can be repeated 2-4 hours later if symptoms persist.  Therapies for angioedema should never delay securing the airway. administration: 1) Remove 40 mL from a 250 mL bag of 0.9% NaCl = 210 mL. 2) Add 2000 mg (40 mL) in 210 mL of 0.9% NaCl. 3) Final concentration = 8 mg/mL. 3) Start the infusion at the indicated rate.  Dose = 10 mg/kg/h with a maximum rate of 650 mg/h.
particularity: Consult your poison control center.  The duration of treatment depends on the symptomatology of the patient.

administration: Presentation: 100mg/mL (10mL vial) 1) Add the calculated dose to 100mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Administer IV over 20 minutes  Stop the infusion if the patient converts to sinus rhythm.

administration: Presentation: 100mg/mL (10mL vial) 1) Add the calculated dose to 100mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Administer IV over 30min  Stop the infusion if the patient converts to sinus rhythm.
particularity: Avoid if systolic BP < 100 mmHg or QTc > 500 msec. Stop infusion if BP drops or QRS lengthens. Verifiy QTc after conversion.  Avoid in patients initially treated with other anti-arrhythmics to avoid excessive QT interval prolongation and torsade de pointes.

administration: Administer IV push
particularity: Monitor for hypotension. In hemodynamically unstable patients, significantly reduce the dose or choose another agent with less hypotensive effect. Consider the co-administration of vasopressors.  There is no consensus in the literature on the maximum dose of propofol. The maximum doses are an editorial choice, based on the fact that propofol is usually dosed with ideal weight.

administration: An initial dose of 1 mg/kg is more effective for procedural amnesia. Subsequent doses of 0.5 mg/kg q3-5min PRN. Adjust according to patient physiology.
particularity: Monitor for hypotension and consider dose adjustment according to age and hemodynamic status. Elderly and/or unstable patients can be sedated at doses as low as 0.1-0.25mg/kg.  There is no consensus in the literature on the maximum dose of propofol. The maximum dose of 80mg is an editorial choice, based on the fact that propofol is usually dosed with ideal weight.

administration: Can be administered without dilution. Administer a first dose of 0.5 to 1mg IV. A dose of 1 to 2mg can be repeated 10 minutes later depending on clinical response. Make the transition to PO therapy as soon as possible.
particularity: Administer with caution in patients with heart failure. Esmolol may potentially be a better option given its short duration of action in those patients at risk for cardiovascular decompensation.  Use the oral route if possible at doses of 20-80mg q4-6h. administration: The 50mg dose of protamine is for patients with major bleeding and with recent (<8h) administration of therapeutic dose enoxaparin only if it is impossible to calculate the exact dose of protamine (see other entries). In case of less severe bleeding or if the administration of the anticoagulant dates more than 8 hours, calculate the appropriate dose of protamine (see other entries) or consider administering a dose of 25mg and repeating the dose only if necessary depending on clinical response (maximum 50mg total).  Overcorrection with protamine can lead to paradoxical bleeding.  Presentation: 10mg/mL (5mL vial) 1) Add the calculated dose to 50mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Administer IV over 10min
particularity: Rapid administration may cause hypotension or an non-IgE mediated anaphylactic reaction.  There is no literature to support the use of fixed-dose protamine in reversing LMWHs. This dose is an editorial choice, to be used only for the patient who is actively bleeding and who has received a dose of LMWH, but in whom the dose and the time of administration are unknown. The 50mg dose is extrapolated from unfractionated heparin reversal data.

administration: The 50mg dose of protamine is for patients with major bleeding and with ongoing or recent administration of therapeutic dose heparin only if it is impossible to calculate the exact dose of protamine (see below). In case of less severe bleeding or if the administration of the anticoagulant dates more than a few hours, calculate the appropriate dose of protamine (see below) or consider administering a dose of 25mg and repeating the dose only if necessary depending on clinical response (maximum 50mg total). Overcorrection with protamine can lead to paradoxical bleeding.  Calculation based on dose of heparin: 1. Calculate the amount of heparin to neutralize: all the units administered in the last hour + half the units administered 2 hrs ago + a quarter of the units administered 3 hours ago. 2. Determine the amount of protamine to administer: quantity of heparin to neutralize / 100. (1mg of protamine neutralizes 100 units of heparin). Maximum 50mg per dose. 3. Presentation: 10mg/mL (5mL vial). 4. Add the calculated dose to 50mL of NaCl 0.9%. 5. Do NOT remove volume. 6. Administer IV over 10min.
particularity: Rapid administration may cause hypotension or a non-IgE mediated anaphylactic reaction.

administration: If the seizures stop during the 10 minute infusion, administer the remainder of the infusion over 4 hours. If the seizures start again during these 4 hours, increase the rate again and administer the remainder of the infusion quickly (less than 10 minutes).  If the seizures continue or recur after the administration of the first dose, administer a second dose in the same manner.
particularity: Treat seizures concomitantly with benzodiazepines.

administration: 1) Add 5mg = 5000mcg (5mL) of IV salbutamol to a 500mL bag of 0.9% NaCl. 2) Final concentration of 10 mcg/mL. 3) Start the infusion at 5mcg/min. 4) Depending on clinical response, increase by 5mcg/min q15-30 minutes. 5) Maximum recommended dose of 20mcg/min.  Off-label: Can be prepared at a higher concentration up to a maximum of 500mcg/mL.
particularity: For patients with acute severe asthma, the inhaled route for bronchodilators is as good as or better than giving the same drugs intravenously. Intravenous administration of bronchodilators should be reserved for patients who are unable to use or who are unresponsive to the inhaled route.

administration: Salbutamol solution 5mg/mL 1. Withdraw 8mL from a 50mL bag of 0,9% NaCl = 42mL. 2. Add 90mg (18mL) of salbutamol to the bag. 3. Final concentration of 90mg in 60mL = 1.5mg/mL. 4. Infuse directly into the nebulizer at 15mg/h = 10mL/h. 5. Administer a 2mL bolus into the nebulizer before connecting oxygen. 6. Adjust the oxygen flow between 5 and 10L/min, preventing the nebulizer from emptying or overflowing.
particularity: Consider in case of severe desaturation between treatments or little/no response to first treatment. administration: Administer preferably through a proximal IV access (hyperosmolar solution).
particularity: Administer in case of severe hyponatraemia with symptoms (seizures, altered level of consciousness, confusion, vomiting, tremors, movement disorders, etc.)  If there is no clinical improvement 5-10 minutes after administration, dose can be repeated up to a total of 300mL. The biochemical objective is to increase serum sodium by 4-6 mmol/L in the first hours of treatment.  Monitor serum sodium closely thereafter, according to usual guidelines and with specialist consultation if necessary. Serum sodium should not increase by more than 6-8 mmol/L per 24 hour period. Risk factors for osmotic demyelination syndrome: serum Na <105 at presentation, chronic/subacute presentation, hypokalemia, malnutrition, advanced liver failure or alcoholism (or history of).  If possible, before administration of 3% saline, measure serum and urine electrolytes/osmolarity.

administration: 12500 mg = 12.5 g Administer undiluted over 10 minutes on a syringe driver.  Repeat as needed for an additional ½ dose (6250 mg) if signs of cyanide toxicity reappear.
particularity: The antidote of choice in the treatment of cyanide poisoning is hydroxocobalamin. Sodium thiosulfate is an adjuvant treatment when there is a partial response to hydroxocobalamin.  Discontinue nitroprusside if in use.

administration: 1) Add 12.5 g (12500 mg = 50 mL) to a 100 mL bag of 0.9% NaCl. 2) Final concentration = 83.3 mg/mL. 3) Administer IV over 10 minutes. 4) Repeat as needed for additional ½ dose (6250 mg) if signs of cyanide toxicity reappear.
particularity: The antidote of choice in the treatment of cyanide poisoning is hydroxocobalamin. Sodium thiosulfate is an adjuvant treatment when there is a partial response to hydroxocobalamin.  Discontinue nitroprusside if in use.

administration: Presentation: 20units/mL (1mL vial) 1) Add 40units (2mL) to 100mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Final concentration: 0.4units/mL 2) Start at 0.03units/min and keep fixed dose (see particularities)
particularity: Doses ranging from 0.01-0.06 units/min have been reported in the literature. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign suggests a fixed dose at 0.03units/min (1.8units/hr).  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.
reference_snippet: - Russell et al. Vasopressin versus Norepinephrine Infusion in Patients with Septic Shock, N Engl J Med 2008; 358:877-887 DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa067373  - Gordon AC, Mason AJ, Thirunavukkarasu N, et al. Effect of Early Vasopressin vs Norepinephrine on Kidney Failure in Patients With Septic Shock: The VA

administration: Presentation: 20units/mL (1mL vial) 1) Add 40units (2mL) to 100mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Final concentration: 0.4units/mL 4) Start infusion at 0.01units/min 5) Titrate by increments of 0.01units/min q15min as needed (t1/2 10min) (Expert opinion)
particularity: There is a possible risk of tissue necrosis, coronary and mesenteric ischemia, especially at doses higher than 0.04units/min; use with caution. There may be rebound hypotension with weaning and withdrawal. Consider slowly tapering by 0.01units/min every 30-60 minutes.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Presentation: 20units/mL (1mL vial) 1) Add 40units (2mL) to 100mL of NaCl 0.9% 2) Do NOT remove volume 3) Final concentration: 0.4units/mL 4) Start infusion at 0.01units/min 5) Titrate by increments of 0.01units/min q15min as needed (t1/2 10min) (Expert opinion)
particularity: There is a possible risk of tissue necrosis, coronary and mesenteric ischemia, especially at doses higher than 0.04units/min; use with caution. There may be rebound hypotension with weaning and withdrawal. Consider slowly tapering by 0.01units/min every 30-60 minutes.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Presentation: 16mg/mL trimethoprim and 80mg/mL sulfamethoxazole.  Dose 5mg/kg expressed in trimethroprim.  Doses of less than 400mg are administered in 250mL of D5% over 60 minutes. Doses of 400mg and more are administered in 500mL of D5% over 90 minutes.  In case of fluid restriction, dilute every 1mL (16mg TMP/ 80mg SMX) in 10mL of solute.
particularity: The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Usual interval q6-8h.  Expert advice: Adjusted body weight is used for all weights (when inferior to total body weight) to ensure a logical progression in the doses with increasing patient weights. Some sources recommend using adjusted body weight only in patients with obesity (total body weight > 120% of ideal body weight or BMI > 30).

administration: Calculate the dose by body weight: 91kg and more: 800 mg 66 to 90kg: 600 mg 40 to 65kg: 400 mg Less than 40kg: 8 mg/kg  1) Choose the vials corresponding to the desired dose and according to availability. 2) Add the total dose to a 100mL bag of 0.9% NaCl. 3) To mix the solution, gently invert the bag to avoid foaming. 4) Administer IV over 1 hour. 5) Finish by rinsing with 20mL of 0.9% NaCl.
particularity: Indications: COVID-19 + with hypoxemia under oxygen therapy with CRP >/= 75 mg/L or high-flow oxygen therapy, non-invasive or invasive mechanical ventilation.  Contraindications: Allergy, septic shock, immunosuppression, liver enzymes more than 5 times upper limit of normal, platelets < 50 x 10e9/L, neutrophils < 0.5 x 10e9/L.  Keep refrigerated.  In case of a tocilizumab shortage, consider using sarilumab.

administration: 1) Reconstitute every 500mg dose with 10mL of NaCl 0.9% for a final concentration of 50mg/mL. 2) Add to the recommended volume of NaCl 0.9%. 3) Administer every 500mg dose in 30 minutes using a volumetric pump. A faster administration can lead to hypersensibility reactions (vancomycin infusion syndrome a.k.a. "red man syndrome").  Maximal concentration for peripheral IV use = 5mg/mL.
particularity: Loading dose in severe infection. Adjust the doses and the interval of subsequent doses according to renal function and indication. Max dose = 3g.

administration: If the POTASSIUM level is low (<3.5mmol/L), use POTASSIUM phosphate preferably, or SODIUM phosphate if not available.  This administration method is for peripheral venous access. For central venous access, a dose of 30mmol can be administered in 100mL of NaCl 0.9%.  Also compatible with D5%.
concentration_note: POTASSIUM or SODIUM phosphate
particularity: Use IV phosphate in patients with:  • Severe hypophosphatemia (< 0.32mmol/L (1mg/dL)) • Moderate hypophosphatemia (0.32 to 0.64mmol/L (1 to 2mg/dL)) AND symptoms (muscle weakness, respiratory failure, neurological symptoms, etc.).  Otherwise, treat with PO phosphate.  Adjust dose in renal insufficiency (ClCr < 30mL/min). Consult a specialist if necessary.  If the phosphate level is below 0.32mmol/L (1mg/dL), consider completing repletion with a second dose of 15mmol in 250mL of 0.9% NaCl over 3 hours.  POTASSIUM phosphate contains 4.4mmol of potassium for every 3mmol of phosphate.  Absolute values ​​are approximate. Rely on values ​​from your local laboratory or local protocols.

A Randomized Noninferiority Trial to Compare Enteral to Parenteral Phosphate Replacement on Biochemistry, Waste, and Environmental Impact and Healthcar

administration: If POTASSIUM level is normal (≥ 3.5mmol/L), use SODIUM Phosphate.  This administration method is for peripheral venous access. For central venous access, a dose of 30mmol can be administered in 100mL of NaCl 0.9%.  Also compatible with D5%.
concentration_note: SODIUM phosphate
particularity: Use IV phosphate in patients with:  • Severe hypophosphatemia (< 0.32mmol/L (1mg/dL)) • Moderate hypophosphatemia (0.32 to 0.64mmol/L (1 to 2mg/dL)) AND symptoms (muscle weakness, respiratory failure, neurological symptoms, etc.).  Otherwise, treat with PO phosphate.  Adjust dose in renal insufficiency (ClCr < 30mL/min). Consult a specialist if necessary.  If the phosphate level is below 0.32mmol/L (1mg/dL), consider completing repletion with a second dose of 15mmol of sodium phosphate in 250mL of 0.9% NaCl over 3 hours.  Absolute values ​​are approximate. Rely on values ​​from your local laboratory or local protocols.

administration: There are few data to support the use of enoxaparin for patients over 150kg. Choose another molecule or administer a dose of 150mg +/- monitor anti-Xa levels or dose according to the actual body weight according to hemorrhagic risk and clinical situation.  Where possible, round to the nearest pre-prepared syringe dose: 40mg, 50mg, 60mg, 70mg, 80mg, 100mg, 120mg, 150mg.
particularity: Adjust dose for patients with ClCr <30 mL/minute: 1mg/kg daily. Contraindicated for patients on renal replacement therapy.  The maximum dose of 150mg is an editorial choice, but supported by guidelines, since the population over 150kg is poorly represented in the studies. Although some guidelines and organizations recommend dosing enoxaparin using total weight at 1mg/kg BID with no upper limit, this may increase bleeding risk and potentially be of no benefit. Indeed, some studies show that doses of 0.7-0.8mg/kg BID are probably as effective as doses of 1mg/kg BID for the morbidly obese population. To be considered according to certain patient-specific factors: hemorrhagic risk, clot burden, age, renal function, BMI. Dosing anti-Xa levels should be considered in order to ensure the effectiveness of the treatment. Alternatively, another drug may be used such as unfractionated heparin or a vitamin K antagonist or a DOAC, depending on the clinical situation.

administration: There are few data to support the use of tinzaparin for patients over 139kg. Choose another molecule or administer a dose of 12,000 units +/- monitor anti-Xa levels or dose according to the actual body weight according to hemorrhagic risk and clinical situation.  Where possible, round to the nearest pre-prepared syringe dose: 2,500 units, 3,500 units, 4,500 units, 8,000 units, 10,000 units, 12,000 units, 14,000 units, 16,000 units, 18,000 units.
particularity: Use with caution for patients with ClCr <30 mL/minute: the monograph recommends a dose reduction, but without defining this reduction.  The maximum dose of 12,000 units BID is an editorial choice, but supported by guidelines, since the obese population is poorly represented in the studies. Although some guidelines and organizations recommend dosing tinzaparin using total body weight and 175 units/kg daily with no upper limit, this may increase bleeding risk and potentially be of no benefit. The monograph recommends limiting the daily dose to 18,000 units. Dosing anti-Xa levels should be considered in order to ensure the effectiveness of the treatment. Alternatively, another drug may be used such as unfractionated heparin or a vitamin K antagonist or a DOAC, depending on the clinical situation.

administration: There are few data to support the use of dalteparin for patients over 150kg. Choose another molecule or administer a dose of 15,000 units +/- monitor anti-Xa levels or dose according to the actual body weight according to hemorrhagic risk and clinical situation.  Where possible, round to the nearest pre-prepared syringe dose: 5,000 units, 7,500 units, 10,000 units, 12,500 units, 15,000 units, 18,000 units.
particularity: Use with caution for patients with ClCr <30 mL/minute, monitoring anti-Xa levels as needed.  The maximum dose of 15,000 units BID is an editorial choice, but supported by guidelines, since the population over 150 kg is poorly represented in the studies. Although some guidelines and organizations recommend dosing dalteparin using a total body weight up to 190kg, this may increase the risk of bleeding and potentially be of no benefit. Also, the monograph recommends a maximum dose of 18,000 units per day, recognizing however that higher doses are probably safe in obesity. Dosing anti-Xa levels should be considered in order to ensure the effectiveness of the treatment. Alternatively, another drug may be used such as unfractionated heparin or a vitamin K antagonist or a DOAC, depending on the clinical situation.

administration: Administer only if fomepizole is not available. Aim for serum ethanol level of 22 mmol/L (measure every 2 hours). All concentrations are in % volume in volume (v/v).   Dilute ethanol in juice in order to obtain a final concentration of approximately 20 % (200 mg/mL).   Suggested preparation method using 99.5 % - 100 % ethanol solution for injection • Put 20 mL of 99.5 % - 100 % ethanol in 80 mL of juice. Final volume: 100 mL. Final concentration: 20 %. • Put 50 mL of 99.5 % - 100 % ethanol in 200 mL of juice. Final volume: 250 mL. Final concentration: 20 %. • Put 100 mL of 99.5 % - 100 % ethanol in 400 mL of juice. Final volume: 500 mL. Final concentration: 20 %.  Suggested preparation method using 40 % oral solution of ethanol (e.g. vodka) • Put 50 mL of 40 % ethanol in 50 mL of juice. Final volume: 100 mL. Final concentration: 20 %. • Put 125 mL of 40 % ethanol in 125 mL of juice. Final volume: 250 mL. Final concentration: 20 %. • Put 250 mL of 40 % ethanol in 250 mL of juice. Final volume: 500 mL. Final concentration: 20 %.
particularity: When fomepizole is not available: Methanol poisoning if serum concentration is equal or greater than 6 mmol/L (19,2 mg/dL). Ethylene glycol poisoning if serum concentration is equal or greater than 3 mmol/L (18,6 mg/dL). Circumstances suggesting one of these poisons pending testing, i.e.: • Metabolic acidosis with increased anion gap and unexplained osmolar gap greater than 10. • History, signs or symptoms suggestive of exposure  On a case-by-case basis for other substances, after consulting with your poison centre.

Antidotes for poisoning by alcohols that form toxic metabolites. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2024 Sep 5. doi: 10.1111/bcp.16232. Epub ahead of print. PMID: 3923

administration: Use only if fomepizole not available. Aim for serum ethanol level of 22 mmol/L (measure every 2 hours). All concentrations are in % volume in volume (v/v).    For IV ethanol, only use commercially available IV ethanol preparation. If not available, use PO ethanol (see other medication entry).   Suggested preparation method using 99.5 - 100 % ethanol solution for injection • Put 100 mL of 99.5 - 100 % ethanol solution for injection in 900 mL of D5W. Final volume: 1000 mL. Final concentration: approx. 10 % = 100 mg/mL. • Use this preparation for the loading dose and the maintenance dose. • Administer using a volumetric pump. • Ideally, ethanol should be administered by central line.

particularity: Subsequent doses q6-8h.

administration: For patients who cannot tolerate the PO route, consider placing and administering via nasogastric tube. Alternatively, intrarectal administration is possible (see references Yeung et al. and Jongjaroenprasert et al.). If this is still not possible, some sources describe how to prepare a solution for intravenous administration (see reference Giles et al.).
particularity: The loading dose is optional, depending on the clinical situation. Some sources recommend starting with maintenance dosing. Subsequent doses: 250mg q4h.  May cause hepatotoxicity and liver failure. Prefer methimazole in less severe cases. Reserve propylthiouracil for fulminant cases or pregnant women in the 1st trimester.  Wait at least one hour after propylthiouracil before administering iodine.

particularity: There is lack of evidence for the use of steroids in the acute management of anaphylaxis. Their use should not be systematic and could be reserved for certain cases (eg bronchospasm, upper airway obstruction, shock). The main treatment remains epinephrine.

administration: The calculated rate is an initial rate based on the modified Brooke/Parkland formula: 2 mL/kg of IV fluids per percentage body surface area burned second or third degree over the first 24 hours, the first half being administered in the first 8 hours.  Adjust rate every hour according to volume status, urine output and hemodynamics. Hypovolemic patients may require initial IV fluid boluses and more aggressive fluid resuscitation.
particularity: Applicable only for patients with 20 % or greater of TBSA burned second or third degree.  Favor balanced fluids (e.g. LR). Consider the addition of albumin for refractory patients. Consider other causes of hypoperfusion and avoid volume overload.

administration: Pre-prepared bag of 10 mmol in 100 mL.  Via peripheral venous access, administer IV with a maximum rate of 20 mmol over 1 hour. Via central venous access, administer IV with a maximum rate of 40 mmol over 1 hour.   In case of pain at the infusion site, reduce infusion rate or administer in "Y" with another infusion.
particularity: PO potassium is usually well absorbed. Reserve IV potassium for refractory or severe cases or if PO administration is impossible.  In severe hypokalemia, potassium deficit can be in the order of 100-200 mmol/L or more. Consider concomitant PO potassium administration as higher doses can be given by the PO route.  Consider serum magnesium measurement and/or magnesium repletion.  Avoid dextrose and insulin administration if possible. Follow potassium levels closely in patients with kidney injury.

administration: Although administration via a central venous access is preferable (hyperosmolar solution), administration via peripheral venous access has been shown to be safe.  May be administered over 3-5 minutes in case of severe intracranial hypertension.
particularity: Measure serum sodium and osmolarity post treatment and avoid Na > 155mmol/L and osmolarity > 320mOsm/kg.
